raw_case:
Double trouble: Necrotizing tonsillitis in the setting of streptococcus pyogenes superinfection in a patient with Epstein-Barr virus
Tonsillitis, the inflammation of the palatine tonsils, is a common cause of throat pain. It is most often due to viruses but can also be related to bacteria. Necrotizing tonsillitis is a rare complication of acute tonsillitis with relatively few cases reported in the literature. We present a case of necrotizing tonsillitis in a patient with mononucleosis related to infection by Epstein-Barr virus, with superinfection with Streptococcus pyogenes, or group A streptococcus. To our knowledge, this is the first case of this nature to be reported in the medical literature. While the role of radiologic imaging is generally of limited diagnostic utility in cases of uncomplicated tonsillitis, its use was invaluable in ascertaining the nature and scope of necrotizing tonsillitis in this case. Further research remains to determine whether viral or bacterial superinfection can be used to further stratify patients who present with sore throat.

Introduction

Throat pain, caused by acute tonsillitis, is a common reason for outpatient medical visit. While most often caused by viral infection, bacterial infection accounts for a significant minority of cases. Complications of acute tonsillitis include peritonsillar abscesses, parapharyngeal and retropharyngeal abscesses, and necrotizing fasciitis [1]. The role of radiologic imaging is limited in cases of uncomplicated tonsillitis; however, it is a useful tool in the characterization of complications of acute tonsillitis [[2], [3], [4]]. Pathogen-specific complications of acute tonsillitis include peritonsillar abscess and Lemierre's syndrome [5]. Necrotizing tonsillitis is a rare complication of acute tonsillitis with relatively few reported cases in the medical literature. We present a case of necrotizing tonsillitis in a patient with mononucleosis related to infection with Epstein-Barr virus and superinfection with GAS.

Case

Patient is a 19-year-old woman with vomiting for 1 day. She was diagnosed with infectious mononucleosis 8 days prior at urgent care when she was having sore throat and fever. Patient was afebrile with otherwise normal vitals on presentation. On physical exam, the oropharynx was gray with areas of necrosis and associated mild right-sided uvular deviation, foul odor, and muffled voice. There was a mildly tender right submandibular lymph node. Laboratory findings were notable for leukocytosis of 10.9 K/UL, positive EBV VCA IgM, positive EBV VCA IgG, positive EBV EA antibody, negative EBNA IgG antibody, and positive rapid Group A streptococci (GAS) antigen throat swab. Computed tomography (CT) of the neck with intravenous (IV) contrast was significant for nonenhancing necrotic tissue replacing the palatine tonsils and extending to the soft palate and tongue base (Fig. 1), consistent with necrotizing tonsillitis. Otolaryngology and Infectious Diseases were consulted, and the patient was treated with IV ampicillin/sulbactam and linezolid. The patient's symptoms improved after 2 days of antibiotics, and the patient was discharged with amoxicillin-clavulanate. No follow up imaging was obtained during this admission.

Discussion

Sore throat, or pharyngitis, is a common medical condition caused by inflammation of the structures of the neck. It accounts for 2%-5% of all outpatient primary care visits in adults and children [6]. The hollow compartments of the neck include the nasopharynx, which runs from the base of the skull to the soft palate, the oropharynx, which extends from the posterior one-third of the tongue to the vallecula, and the larynx, which extends to the margin of the cricoid cartilage [7].The palatine tonsils are a prominent lymphoid structure located in the oropharynx and are a major component of Waldeyer's ring [8]. Inflammation of the tonsils causing tonsillitis is a common cause of throat pain. Tonsillitis can occur via direct invasion by pathogens, or due to irritation by nasal secretions [7]. Viral infection is the most common cause of tonsillitis, with reports of infection by Cytomegalovirus (CMV), toxoplasmosis, Human Immunodeficiency Syndrome (HIV), Hepatitis A virus (HAV), rubella, and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) [9]. While bacterial tonsillitis is less common, the most common bacterial pathogen responsible is Streptococcus pyogenes, or GAS, with other less common causes including Group C and Group G streptococci, Haemophilus influenzae, Nocardia, Corynebacterium, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Fusobacterium necrophorum [10]. Infection is usually transmitted via droplets although autoinfection via normal flora can also occur [9,10].Supportive care is the cornerstone of treatment for acute tonsillitis since many cases are due to viral etiologies. Antibiotics are used in acute tonsillitis due to bacterial causes. There is little data for the management of necrotizing tonsillitis, but tonsillectomy is often needed [9]. Airway compromise can lead to severe outcomes such as death [11].The role of radiologic imaging is generally of limited diagnostic utility in cases of uncomplicated tonsillitis, however its use is invaluable in cases where there is suspicion for complication, as differentiation between uncomplicated and complicated tonsillitis is relatively poor when using clinical metrics alone [4]. In the setting of severe sore throat with associated fever and chills, cervical lymphadenopathy, trismus, dysphagia, and odynophagia, imaging modalities such as CT, and less commonly ultrasound, are necessary for determining presence of acute complications and defining their nature and scope [[2], [3], [4]].As acute tonsillitis is a frequent reason for emergency room visits, there are many published works identifying the causes and complications of acute tonsillitis, including necrotizing fasciitis and other deep neck infections; however, there is a relative paucity of reported cases describing necrotizing tonsillitis, in which the lymphoid tissues of the tonsils undergo necrosis. Of those reported cases, the implicated pathogens have been Group C β-hemolytic streptococci [12] and herpes simplex virus (HSV) (Bohran) [13]. To our knowledge, this is the first reported case of necrotizing tonsillitis in the context of EBV infection. While there has been a reported case of hemorrhagic tonsillitis associated with EBV [14], this case is of significant interest given the superinfection of GAS on EBV.Beyond the scope of this case report, more research is needed to ascertain if bacterial superinfection is associated with necrotizing tonsillitis in patients with concomitant infection with EBV, or tonsillitis caused by other viruses. If so, risk factors of patients may be identified to stratify patients who present with sore throat. This can further guide diagnostic and treatment regimens in these patients to improve clinical outcomes.

Conclusion

We presented a case of necrotizing tonsillitis in 19-year-old women with EBV infectious mononucleosis found to have GAS superinfection. Additional research is needed to identify high risk patient groups who may have poor clinical outcomes.

Patient consent

Consent for publication of the case was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 19-year-old woman  
- **Chief Complaint:** Vomiting for 1 day  
- **History of Present Illness:** Sore throat and fever 8 days prior, diagnosed with infectious mononucleosis at urgent care. Current symptoms include throat pain, a gray oropharynx with necrosis, uvular deviation, foul odor, and a muffled voice.  
- **Past Medical History:** Infectious mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr virus [EBV])  
- **Personal History:** None provided  
- **Family History:** None provided  
- **Physical Examination:** Afebrile, mild right-sided uvular deviation, foul odor in oropharynx, muffled voice, mildly tender right submandibular lymph node.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
   - Leukocytosis (WBC count: 10.9 K/UL)  
   - Positive EBV VCA IgM, VCA IgG, and EBV EA antibody  
   - Negative EBNA IgG antibody  
   - Positive rapid Group A streptococcus (GAS) antigen throat swab  
   - CT neck with IV contrast: Nonenhancing necrotic tonsillar tissue extending to soft palate and tongue base  

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial differential diagnoses based on symptoms:**  
   - Acute tonsillitis  
   - Peritonsillar abscess  
   - Necrotizing fasciitis affecting tonsils  
   - Infectious mononucleosis with tonsillar complications  

2. **Assessment and exclusion:**  
   - **Acute tonsillitis**: Initial sore throat could suggest uncomplicated tonsillitis; however, the necrotic tissue on CT and gray appearance of oropharynx with foul odor exclude this as a sole diagnosis.  
   - **Peritonsillar abscess**: CT imaging did not show localized fluid collection or abscess formation.  
   - **Necrotizing fasciitis affecting tonsils**: Necrosis involving tonsillar tissue supports this, though typically more severe systemic toxicity is observed.  
   - **Infectious mononucleosis with tonsillar complications**: EBV serology confirms mononucleosis, consistent with patient's initial diagnosis 8 days earlier. Secondary bacterial complications (GAS superinfection confirmed via rapid antigen test) point toward a rare complication—necrotizing tonsillitis.  

3. **Definitive diagnosis:** Necrotizing tonsillitis was confirmed based on CT findings (nonenhancing necrotic tissue involving tonsils) and the combination of EBV mononucleosis alongside GAS superinfection.  

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Necrotizing tonsillitis in the setting of Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infectious mononucleosis with group A streptococcus (GAS) superinfection.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Necrotizing tonsillitis due to EBV mononucleosis and GAS superinfection.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to figure out what's going on with this patient. So, she's a 19-year-old woman who came in with vomiting for one day. But her history shows she had a sore throat and fever eight days ago, and was diagnosed with infectious mononucleosis. Now, her current symptoms include throat pain, a gray oropharynx with necrosis, uvular deviation, foul odor, and a muffled voice.

Hmm, infectious mononucleosis is caused by EBV, right? The lab tests here confirm that with positive VCA IgM and IgG, and EA antibody, but negative EBNA IgG. That makes sense for a recent EBV infection because EBNA IgG usually appears later. Also, she tested positive for Group A strep. So maybe she has both mono and strep throat? But wait, mono can sometimes cause a false positive strep test, but the rapid antigen test is pretty specific. So she might have a co-infection.

Now, the physical exam shows she's afebrile now, which is interesting. But she has a muffled voice, uvular deviation, and necrotic tissue on the CT scan. The CT shows nonenhancing necrotic tissue extending to the soft palate and tongue base. That makes me think of some kind of abscess or necrotizing infection. Like, maybe a peritonsillar abscess? But those typically present with severe throat pain, trismus, and a hot potato voice. Her uvular deviation to the right might indicate a right-sided abscess pushing the uvula to the opposite side.

Wait, but she also has necrotic tissue. Necrotizing infections like necrotizing fasciitis or Lemierre's syndrome come to mind. Lemierre's is usually caused by Fusobacterium, often following a pharyngitis, and leads to septic thrombophlebitis of the jugular vein. But she's young, and with recent mono, which can predispose to bacterial complications. The CT showing necrosis in the tonsillar area extending to the soft palate and tongue base—maybe that's more than just a peritonsillar abscess. Could it be a deep neck infection with necrosis, like necrotizing tonsillitis?

Also, she has leukocytosis (WBC 10.9), which is elevated but not extremely high. In mono, the WBC can be elevated with lymphocytosis, but here the presence of strep might contribute to neutrophilia. The combination of EBV and strep could be leading to a more severe infection. But the necrosis on CT is concerning. Necrotizing infections can be life-threatening and require urgent intervention.

Another angle: Infectious mononucleosis can cause significant pharyngitis and tonsillar enlargement, which might lead to obstructive symptoms. However, the necrosis and foul odor suggest tissue death, which isn't typical for mono alone. So maybe a secondary bacterial infection causing necrotizing tonsillitis or abscess formation. Group A strep can cause necrotizing fasciitis, though that's more common in the extremities. But in the throat, maybe a severe invasive strep infection complicating mono.

Wait, but she's also had a positive rapid strep test. So treatment for strep would usually be penicillin or amoxicillin, but in mono, amoxicillin can cause a rash. If she was given amoxicillin, maybe she developed a rash, but that's not mentioned here. The main issue now is the necrosis and possible abscess.

Putting it all together: The key findings are the necrotic tonsillar tissue with extension, uvular deviation, foul odor, and muffled voice. These are signs of a deep neck space infection with necrosis. The most likely diagnosis here is a complication of her EBV infection, possibly a peritonsillar abscess, but the necrosis suggests something more severe. Alternatively, it could be a retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal abscess with necrotizing features. However, considering the CT findings, maybe it's a necrotizing tonsillitis or an abscess with tissue necrosis. Another possibility is Ludwig's angina, but that typically involves the sublingual and submandibular spaces bilaterally, which isn't mentioned here.

Wait, the CT shows necrosis extending to the soft palate and tongue base. That's more consistent with a spreading necrotizing infection. Given the recent EBV infection which can compromise the immune response and the presence of Group A strep, it's possible that she has developed a severe, necrotizing bacterial infection complicating her mono. The combination of EBV and bacterial infection could lead to such tissue destruction.

Another thought: Epstein-Barr virus can sometimes lead to secondary infections due to the immune suppression it causes. The presence of both EBV and GAS could be leading to a synergistic infection. But the imaging showing necrosis makes me think more of an aggressive bacterial process. The term "necrotizing tonsillitis" might apply here, but the extension into the soft palate and tongue base suggests a deep neck infection. The term "Lemierre's syndrome" involves septic thrombophlebitis, but the CT didn't mention jugular vein thrombosis. However, maybe that wasn't imaged, or it's early.

Alternatively, could this be a case of acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG), but that usually affects the gums, and the description here is more of the oropharynx. The foul odor is common in ANUG, but the location here is tonsillar and palate.

Considering the CT findings of non-enhancing necrotic tissue, perhaps this is a retropharyngeal abscess with necrosis. But the key is that the patient has signs of a severe infection with tissue necrosis in the setting of recent EBV and current strep infection. The combination of these infections could lead to such a presentation.

So, the final diagnosis here is likely a complication of infectious mononucleosis with a secondary bacterial infection causing a necrotizing process. The most specific term would be "necrotizing tonsillitis" or "necrotizing pharyngitis", but given the CT findings of extension into surrounding tissues, maybe "necrotizing cellulitis" or "deep neck space infection with necrosis". However, the presence of a positive Group A strep suggests that this is a bacterial superinfection complicating the viral illness.

Alternatively, peritonsillar abscess with necrotic tissue. But peritonsillar abscesses are usually localized, whereas the CT here shows extension. So perhaps it's a more severe form, like a parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess with necrosis. But the term that best fits the combination of EBV and necrotizing bacterial infection might be "necrotizing tonsillitis secondary to Group A streptococcus complicating infectious mononucleosis".

Wait, but the answer needs to be a concise diagnosis. The key elements are the EBV infection, the presence of Group A strep, and the necrotic tissue on CT. The combination of these likely leads to a severe bacterial infection complicating mono. In such cases, the diagnosis is often referred to as "severe bacterial tonsillopharyngitis with necrosis complicating infectious mononucleosis" but perhaps more specifically as a "peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing features". However, the CT shows extension beyond the tonsils, so maybe "necrotizing cellulitis of the neck" or "necrotizing fasciitis of the head and neck".

Alternatively, considering the CT findings, the term "Lemierre's syndrome" comes to mind, but that typically involves Fusobacterium necrophorum, not Group A strep. However, Lemierre's is characterized by septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein following a throat infection. The patient here doesn't have mentioned thrombosis, so maybe that's not it. 

Another angle: The patient has infectious mononucleosis, which can cause exudative tonsillitis. The presence of Group A strep would be a co-infection. However, the necrosis suggests tissue death due to a severe bacterial infection. The combination of these two could lead to a rapidly progressing necrotizing infection. The imaging showing non-enhancing tissue suggests necrosis, which is concerning for a life-threatening infection like necrotizing fasciitis. However, in the neck, this is rare but possible. 

The key here is to recognize that the patient has a deep neck infection with necrosis, which can be a complication of both EBV and bacterial infections. The most accurate diagnosis in this scenario would be a "necrotizing tonsillitis" or "necrotizing pharyngitis" with possible extension into the deep neck spaces. But the presence of Group A strep and EBV would point towards a bacterial superinfection causing necrosis. 

Alternatively, the term "deep neck abscess with necrotizing infection" could be used, but I think the most precise diagnosis here is "peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing infection", given the uvular deviation and CT findings. However, peritonsillar abscesses usually have trismus, which isn't mentioned here. The patient's muffled voice and uvular deviation are classic signs of peritonsillar abscess. The necrosis on CT might indicate that the abscess has led to tissue necrosis, complicating the presentation. 

But wait, the CT shows nonenhancing necrotic tissue extending to the soft palate and tongue base. That suggests that the infection has spread beyond the tonsil, which could be a parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess. These deep neck abscesses can be life-threatening and require urgent drainage. 

So, putting it all together, the patient has a history of EBV mononucleosis, which predisposes her to bacterial infections. She now presents with a severe throat infection with necrosis, positive for Group A strep. The CT shows extension of the necrosis, so the diagnosis is most likely a deep neck abscess (like parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal) with necrotizing infection secondary to Group A streptococcus, complicating infectious mononucleosis. However, the specific diagnosis would be the abscess type. Alternatively, if there's extensive necrosis without a localized abscess, necrotizing fasciitis. 

But given that the CT shows nonenhancing necrotic tissue in the tonsillar area extending to surrounding structures, and given the physical exam findings, the most accurate diagnosis is a peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing infection. However, the extension beyond the tonsil may make it a more extensive process. 

Alternatively, the presence of necrotic tonsillar tissue could suggest "necrotizing tonsillitis", which can occur in severe bacterial infections, especially in immunocompromised patients. But in this case, the patient is immunocompetent except for the recent EBV infection, which can transiently suppress the immune system. 

In conclusion, the combination of EBV infection, Group A strep, and the imaging findings of necrosis extending into surrounding tissues points towards a severe, necrotizing bacterial infection complicating infectious mononucleosis. The final diagnosis would be a necrotizing bacterial tonsillitis or a deep neck abscess with necrosis. However, based on the CT findings and clinical presentation, the most precise diagnosis is likely a peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing features. Alternatively, the term "necrotizing cellulitis" of the neck might be used. 

Wait, but the answer needs to be the specific diagnosis. The key here is that the CT shows necrotic tissue extending beyond the tonsils, and she has signs of deep infection. The combination of EBV and GAS with necrosis suggests a severe, invasive bacterial infection. Therefore, the diagnosis is likely a "necrotizing bacterial pharyngitis/tonsillitis complicating infectious mononucleosis". However, another possible term is "Lemierre-like syndrome" but without the jugular vein thrombosis. Alternatively, "deep neck space infection with necrotizing fasciitis". 

But given the CT findings and the clinical presentation, the most accurate diagnosis is a peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing infection. But if the abscess has extended, maybe it's a parapharyngeal abscess. However, the answer might be more specific. Alternatively, considering the imaging, the diagnosis is "necrotizing tonsillitis" or "necrotizing pharyngitis" due to Group A streptococcus superimposed on EBV infectious mononucleosis. 

But to match standard terminology, perhaps the answer is "Peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing infection". However, peritonsillar abscess is a specific diagnosis. Alternatively, "necrotizing tonsillitis". Alternatively, "deep neck infection with necrosis". 

Wait, in the CT, it's extending to the soft palate and tongue base. That suggests that the infection is spreading. The presence of necrosis in the tonsillar tissue and adjacent areas is concerning for a necrotizing infection. So the final diagnosis would be "necrotizing cellulitis of the neck" or "necrotizing fasciitis of the head and neck", but that's more rare. However, given the CT findings and the clinical context, the most likely diagnosis is a "necrotizing tonsillitis with extension into the deep neck spaces", but the precise term might be "necrotizing pharyngotonsillitis complicating infectious mononucleosis". 

Alternatively, since she has a positive Group A strep, maybe "Group A streptococcal necrotizing pharyngitis complicating infectious mononucleosis". That seems specific. But is there a standard term for this? Maybe "severe necrotizing bacterial tonsillitis" or "necrotizing tonsillitis secondary to Group A Streptococcus in a patient with infectious mononucleosis". 

But according to standard medical terminology, such a condition might be referred to as "necrotizing tonsillitis" or "necrotizing pharyngitis", especially given the CT evidence of necrosis. Therefore, the final answer is likely "Necrotizing tonsillitis" or "Necrotizing pharyngotonsillitis". However, considering the rapid strep test positive, it's caused by Group A Streptococcus. So the diagnosis would be "Group A streptococcal necrotizing tonsillitis complicating infectious mononucleosis". 

But the answer needs to be concise. The most precise diagnosis considering all factors is likely "Necrotizing tonsillitis due to Group A Streptococcus and Epstein-Barr virus co-infection". However, in practice, the primary diagnosis would be the complication, so maybe "Peritonsillar abscess with necrosis" or "Deep neck abscess with necrotizing infection". 

But given the CT findings, which mention nonenhancing necrotic tonsillar tissue extending to soft palate and tongue base, the radiologic description is more consistent with necrotizing tonsillitis with extension. Therefore, the final diagnosis is "Necrotizing tonsillitis" or "Necrotizing pharyngotonsillitis". 

Alternatively, in the context of EBV and GAS, perhaps "Severe necrotizing bacterial tonsillitis complicating infectious mononucleosis". But the answer is likely a specific term. Maybe "Lemierre's syndrome", but without the jugular vein thrombosis. 

Wait, Lemierre's is characterized by septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein, typically caused by Fusobacterium. This patient has Group A strep and EBV, so that's different. So Lemierre's is unlikely. 

Alternatively, the diagnosis could be "Acute necrotizing ulcerative tonsillitis", which is a rare condition often associated with severe bacterial infections. Given the CT findings and clinical presentation, this seems plausible. 

In conclusion, after considering all the factors, the most accurate diagnosis is "Necrotizing tonsillitis secondary to Group A Streptococcus complicating infectious mononucleosis". But to match standard terminology, maybe "Necrotizing tonsillitis" is sufficient. Alternatively, "Peritonsillar abscess with necrotizing infection". However, the CT shows extension beyond the tonsils, so perhaps "Parapharyngeal abscess with necrotizing infection". 

But given that the CT mentions the tonsillar tissue is necrotic and extending to soft palate and tongue base, the primary site is the tonsil with spread. Therefore, the most precise diagnosis is "Necrotizing tonsillitis with deep neck extension".
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a confirmed EBV infection (positive EBV VCA IgM, VCA IgG, EA antibody) and a concurrent Group A streptococcus (GAS) infection (positive rapid antigen test), indicating a co-infection.  
<step 2> Clinical findings (gray necrotic oropharynx, uvular deviation, foul odor, muffled voice) and CT evidence of necrotic tonsillar tissue extending to the soft palate and tongue base suggest a severe, invasive bacterial process.  
<step 3> Necrotizing tonsillitis is strongly supported by the combination of EBV-induced immunosuppression, bacterial superinfection (GAS), and imaging showing tissue necrosis.  
<step 4> The absence of jugular vein thrombosis or systemic sepsis rules out Lemierre’s syndrome, while the localized necrosis and deep tissue involvement differentiate it from a simple peritonsillar abscess.  

### Answer:
Necrotizing tonsillitis secondary to Group A Streptococcus complicating infectious mononucleosis

reasoning_eval.step: The patient has a confirmed EBV infection (positive EBV VCA IgM, VCA IgG, EA antibody) and a concurrent Group A streptococcus (GAS) infection (positive rapid antigen test), indicating a co-infection.

Clinical findings (gray necrotic oropharynx, uvular deviation, foul odor, muffled voice) and CT evidence of necrotic tonsillar tissue extending to the soft palate and tongue base suggest a severe, invasive bacterial process.
Necrotizing tonsillitis is strongly supported by the combination of EBV-induced immunosuppression, bacterial superinfection (GAS), and imaging showing tissue necrosis.
The absence of jugular vein thrombosis or systemic sepsis rules out Lemierre’s syndrome, while the localized necrosis and deep tissue involvement differentiate it from a simple peritonsillar abscess.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial differential diagnoses based on symptoms include acute tonsillitis, peritonsillar abscess, necrotizing fasciitis affecting tonsils, and infectious mononucleosis with tonsillar complications.
Step 2: Acute tonsillitis is excluded due to necrotic tissue on CT and the gray appearance of the oropharynx with a foul odor.
Step 3: Peritonsillar abscess is excluded as CT imaging did not show localized fluid collection or abscess formation.
Step 4: Necrotizing fasciitis affecting tonsils is supported by necrosis involving tonsillar tissue, although typically more severe systemic toxicity is observed.
Step 5: Infectious mononucleosis with tonsillar complications is confirmed by EBV serology, consistent with the patient's initial diagnosis 8 days earlier.
Step 6: Secondary bacterial complications, specifically GAS superinfection confirmed via rapid antigen test, indicate a rare complication—necrotizing tonsillitis.
Step 7: Definitive diagnosis is necrotizing tonsillitis based on CT findings (nonenhancing necrotic tissue involving tonsils) and the combination of EBV mononucleosis alongside GAS superinfection.
